era_context: Late Bronze Age, 18th Dynasty Egypt. Mycenaean Late Helladic IIIA. Shang Dynasty China. Hittite Empire in Anatolia. A period of imperial consolidation and long-distance trade across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.

events.title: Death of Thutmose III and succession of Amenhotep II
events.description: Thutmose III, one of Egypt's greatest military pharaohs, died and was succeeded by his son Amenhotep II. The transition of power was likely smooth, as Amenhotep II had been co-regent or designated heir. Amenhotep II inherited a vast empire and continued his father's policies of military consolidation and territorial control. The exact date of Thutmose III's death is debated, with some scholars placing it c. 1425 BCE, others c. 1420 BCE or later.

sources.name: Karnak Temple inscriptions and king-list synchronization
Manetho's Aegyptiaca, preserved in later sources

events.certainty_note: The death of Thutmose III is traditionally dated c. 1425 BCE, but some scholars argue for c. 1420 BCE or even later. The dating relies on king-list synchronization and astronomical calculations, with uncertainty of ±5–10 years. This year is assigned by convention, but the actual date may differ.

events.title: Mycenaean palatial civilization at its height
events.description: Mycenaean palace centers (Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos) were at the height of their administrative and economic power. Extensive trade networks connected the Aegean to Egypt, the Levant, and Anatolia. Pottery production, metalworking, and long-distance commerce were flourishing. This period is well-attested archaeologically through pottery typology and stratigraphic evidence.

sources.name: Archaeological stratigraphy from Mycenaean palace sites

events.certainty_note: Late Helladic IIIA is well-established archaeologically but not precisely dated to individual years. Chronological placement relies on stratigraphic correlation with Egyptian chronology, with margins of ±20–30 years.

events.title: Shang Dynasty oracle bone divination
events.description: The Shang court continued its practice of oracle bone divination. Inscriptions on bones and shells record divination questions and results, providing evidence of the king's concerns regarding weather, harvests, military campaigns, and ancestral communication. Oracle bones are among the earliest written records in China.
events.certainty_note: Oracle bones are contemporary evidence, but individual bones are difficult to date to specific years. Chronological placement within the Shang period relies on stratigraphic context and king-list synchronization. Shang chronology is debated, with uncertainty of ±50–100 years for this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Thutmose III's death is contested. Traditional chronology places it c. 1425 BCE, but some scholars argue for c. 1420 BCE or even later (c. 1410 BCE in some frameworks). This affects whether -1425 represents the year of his death or a year during his reign. Mycenaean chronology is also debated; some scholars argue for a higher chronology that would shift Late Helladic IIIA dates by 20–30 years. Shang chronology remains one of the most disputed areas in Chinese history, with competing frameworks differing by over a century for this period.
historiographic_note: Documentation for this year is sparse. The death of Thutmose III is a significant event, but the exact date is uncertain. Mycenaean evidence is primarily archaeological; written records (Linear B) do not begin until c. 1400 BCE. Shang oracle bones provide unique insight into ritual and divination but are difficult to date precisely. The surviving sources reflect the interests of elite scribal traditions in Egypt, the Levant, and China; the vast majority of human activity is invisible. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are absent from the historical record for this year.

graph_edges.note: Amenhotep II's succession led to continued Egyptian imperial consolidation and military campaigns in the Levant
Mycenaean palatial networks of this period continued and intensified, eventually documented in Linear B records c. 1400 BCE
